title: Ensure that the --audit-log-path argument is set

input_row.title: Ensure that the --audit-log-path argument is set
input_row.description: Enable auditing on the Kubernetes API Server and set the desired audit log path.
input_row.rationale: Auditing the Kubernetes API Server provides a security-relevant chronological set of records documenting the sequence of activities that have affected the system by individual users, administrators or other components of the system. Even though currently, Kubernetes provides only basic audit capabilities, it should be enabled. You can enable it by setting an appropriate audit log path.
input_row.audit: OpenShift audit works at the API server level, logging all requests coming to the server. API server audit is on by default. Run the following command to find the Kubernetes API audit log path: oc get configmap config -n openshift-kube-apiserver -ojson | jq -r '.data["config.yaml"]' | jq '.apiServerArguments["audit-log-path"]' Verify the path is /var/log/kube-apiserver/audit.log . Use the following to verify the audit log exists: export POD=$(oc get pods -n openshift-kube-apiserver -l app=openshift-kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') oc rsh -n openshift-kube-apiserver -c kube-apiserver $POD ls /var/log/kube-apiserver/audit.log echo $? Verify a return code of 0 . Run the following command to find the OpenShift API audit log path: oc get configmap config -n openshift-apiserver -ojson | jq -r '.data["config.yaml"]' | jq '.apiServerArguments["audit-log-path"]' Verify the path is /var/log/openshift-apiserver/audit.log . Use the following to verify the audit log exists: export POD=$(oc get pods -n openshift-apiserver -l apiserver=true -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') oc rsh -n openshift-apiserver $POD ls /var/log/openshift-apiserver/audit.log echo $? Verify a return code of 0 .
input_row.remediation: None required. This is managed by the cluster apiserver operator.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Open the IBM Cloud Console and navigate to the OpenShift cluster you want to audit.
2. Access the OpenShift command line interface (CLI) if not already configured. Use `oc login` with your cluster credentials.
3. Run the following command to check the audit log path for the Kubernetes API Server:
   ```
   oc get configmap config -n openshift-kube-apiserver -ojson | jq -r '.data["config.yaml"]' | jq '.apiServerArguments["audit-log-path"]'
   ```
4. Validate that the output shows the path `/var/log/kube-apiserver/audit.log`.
5. To check if the audit log file exists, retrieve the pod name of the kube-apiserver by executing:
   ```
   export POD=$(oc get pods -n openshift-kube-apiserver -l app=openshift-kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
   ```
6. Then, run the following command to see if the audit log file exists:
   ```
   oc rsh -n openshift-kube-apiserver -c kube-apiserver $POD ls /var/log/kube-apiserver/audit.log
   ```
7. Check the command's return code using `echo $?`. A return code of `0` indicates that the log file exists.
8. Repeat steps 3-7 for the OpenShift API audit log by changing the namespace to `openshift-apiserver`:
   - Run `oc get configmap config -n openshift-apiserver -ojson | jq -r '.data["config.yaml"]' | jq '.apiServerArguments["audit-log-path"]'`.
   - Validate that it outputs the path `/var/log/openshift-apiserver/audit.log` and check if the audit log file exists similarly.

**Expected Secure Configuration:**
- For both API servers, the audit log path should be as follows:
  - Kubernetes API Server: `/var/log/kube-apiserver/audit.log`
  - OpenShift API Server: `/var/log/openshift-apiserver/audit.log`
- Both log files must exist, confirmed by a return code of `0`.

**How to Confirm Compliance:**
- If both audit log paths and their respective log files exist, the control is compliant. If either of the log paths does not exist or if the log files are absent, remedial action should be taken.
